title: Sharon, Israel

infobox.value: ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): הַשָּׁרוֹן, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *HaSharon*; [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): سهل الشارون, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Sahl al-Shārūn*) is the central section of the [Israeli coastal plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain"). The name Sharon is derived from the Akkadian word "A-Sharanu" which literally translates to a "thick forest," which the area was until its deforestation in the late 19th century. The plain lies between the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to the west and the [Samarian Hills](/wiki/Samaria#Geographical_location "Samaria"), 15 km (9.3 mi) to the east. It stretches from [Nahal Taninim](/wiki/Nahal_Taninim "Nahal Taninim"), a stream marking the southern end of [Mount Carmel](/wiki/Mount_Carmel,_Israel "Mount Carmel, Israel") in the north, to the [Yarkon River](/wiki/Yarkon_River "Yarkon River") in the south, at the northern limit of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), over a total of about 90 km (56 mi). The level of the Sharon plain is connected to the level of the Mediterranean Sea by the [Sharon Escarpment](/wiki/Sharon_Escarpment "Sharon Escarpment").

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [32°24′00″N 34°52′59″E﻿ / ﻿32.400°N 34.883°E﻿ / 32.400; 34.883](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Sharon_plain&params=32.400_N_34.883_E_region:IL_type:_landmarksource:dewiki)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Sharon, Israel](/w/index.php?title=Sharon,_Israel&redirect=no "Sharon, Israel"))

Central section of the coastal plain of Israel

[32°24′00″N 34°52′59″E﻿ / ﻿32.400°N 34.883°E﻿ / 32.400; 34.883](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Sharon_plain&params=32.400_N_34.883_E_region:IL_type:_landmarksource:dewiki)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/WV_Sharon_plain.png/250px-WV_Sharon_plain.png)](/wiki/File:WV_Sharon_plain.png)

Sharon plain in Israeli Coastal Plain region

The **Sharon plain** ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): הַשָּׁרוֹן, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *HaSharon*; [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): سهل الشارون, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Sahl al-Shārūn*) is the central section of the [Israeli coastal plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain"). The name Sharon is derived from the Akkadian word "A-Sharanu" which literally translates to a "thick forest," which the area was until its deforestation in the late 19th century. The plain lies between the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to the west and the [Samarian Hills](/wiki/Samaria#Geographical_location "Samaria"), 15 km (9.3 mi) to the east. It stretches from [Nahal Taninim](/wiki/Nahal_Taninim "Nahal Taninim"), a stream marking the southern end of [Mount Carmel](/wiki/Mount_Carmel,_Israel "Mount Carmel, Israel") in the north, to the [Yarkon River](/wiki/Yarkon_River "Yarkon River") in the south, at the northern limit of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), over a total of about 90 km (56 mi). The level of the Sharon plain is connected to the level of the Mediterranean Sea by the [Sharon Escarpment](/wiki/Sharon_Escarpment "Sharon Escarpment").

Parts of the plain are included in the [Central](/wiki/Central_District_(Israel) "Central District (Israel)"), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa_District "Haifa District") and [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_District "Tel Aviv District") [Districts of Israel](/wiki/Districts_of_Israel "Districts of Israel").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early")]

The Sharon valley is mentioned in an [ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") stele of [Amenhotep II](/wiki/Amenhotep_II "Amenhotep II"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) and as the Sharon field containing both [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") and [Dor](/wiki/Tel_Dor "Tel Dor") on the [Sarcophagus of Eshmunazar II](/wiki/Sarcophagus_of_Eshmunazar_II "Sarcophagus of Eshmunazar II").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The Plain of Sharon is mentioned in the Bible ([1 Chronicles](/wiki/1_Chronicles "1 Chronicles") 5:16, 27:29; [Book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah") 33:9, 35:2, 65:10), including the famous reference to the enigmatic "[Rose of Sharon](/wiki/Rose_of_Sharon "Rose of Sharon")" ([Song of Songs 2:1](/wiki/Song_of_Songs_2:1 "Song of Songs 2:1")).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Excavations were performed before road construction in the north part of Sharon plain. Near [En Esur](/wiki/En_Esur "En Esur"), an early [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") planned metropolis, including a temple, stretching over 65 [ha](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") for 6,000 inhabitants, was discovered. Under the 5000-year-old city, an even older settlement from 7000 [YBP](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present") has been found, according to a report from the antiquities office of Israel on 6 October 2019.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The area was historically a thick oak forest[[5]](#cite_note-5) and was described as such by accounts written during the [Battle of Arsuf](/wiki/Battle_of_Arsuf "Battle of Arsuf"), a battle between the forces of [Richard the Lionheart](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") and [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") during the [Third Crusade](/wiki/Third_Crusade "Third Crusade"). The battle took place in the "Forest of Arsuf" in the Sharon Plain.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern")]

Before the 20th century, the region was covered by the Forest of Sharon, an open [woodland](/wiki/Woodland "Woodland") dominated by Mount Tabor oak *([Quercus ithaburensis](/wiki/Quercus_ithaburensis "Quercus ithaburensis"))*, which extended from [Kfar Yona](/wiki/Kfar_Yona "Kfar Yona") in the north to [Ra'ananna](/wiki/Ra%27anana "Ra'anana") in the south. The area was called al-Ghāba in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), "The forest, e.g. the great Oak forest of Sharon".[[7]](#cite_note-7) The local Arab inhabitants traditionally used the area for [pasture](/wiki/Pasture "Pasture"), [firewood](/wiki/Firewood "Firewood") and intermittent [cultivation](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture").[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) In 1851/2, [van de Velde](/wiki/Charles_William_Meredith_van_de_Velde "Charles William Meredith van de Velde") visited the plain of Sharon, and wrote: "The ground was particularly soft and swampy, owing to the waters that flow into it from the hills to the east during and aftter the rains. What a treasure all that water must be, considering the drought that generally parches this land! But still no general blessing follows. Only a few [fellahins](/wiki/Fellah "Fellah") who, with their black tents of goats' hair, lie skulking in the hollows between the low hills, make any use of it."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") of the region began during the rule of the Egyptian Ibrahim Pasha in the 19th century, who had ordered that the oaks be razed so their wood could be used for heating and construction. Later, throughout [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the oaks were cut off to provide heating for the engines of Turkish trains of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") that passed by.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

81% of all Arab settlement in the Plain of Sharon were constructed on ancient sites, indicating significant continuity in the patterns of settlement from earlier periods.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Prior to 1948, the region was subordinate to [Jaffa Subdistrict](/wiki/Jaffa_Subdistrict,_Mandatory_Palestine "Jaffa Subdistrict, Mandatory Palestine") and [Tulkarm Subdistrict](/wiki/Tulkarm_Subdistrict,_Mandatory_Palestine "Tulkarm Subdistrict, Mandatory Palestine"). Historically, while some parts of the Sharon plain were very fertile, much of it was swampy and malarial, a condition exacerbated by massive [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") deforestation. [Zionist](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") immigrants arrived in the early 20th century and populated the region with many settlements.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

By 1945, [Jaffa Subdistrict](/wiki/Jaffa_Subdistrict,_Mandatory_Palestine "Jaffa Subdistrict, Mandatory Palestine") had a population of 373,800, consisting of 71% Jewish residents and 29% Palestinian Muslim and Christian residents. [Tulkarm Subdistrict](/wiki/Tulkarm_Subdistrict,_Mandatory_Palestine "Tulkarm Subdistrict, Mandatory Palestine") had a population 86,120, consisting of 17% Jewish residents and 83% Palestinian Muslim and Christian residents. During the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), the [Arab population](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_villages_depopulated_during_the_1947%E2%80%931949_Palestine_war "List of towns and villages depopulated during the 1947–1949 Palestine war") of the region [fled](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight") or were expelled almost entirely.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2008, it was the most densely populated region of Israel.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Archaeology")]

The Sharon Plain has been the focus of extensive archaeological excavations, revealing a long sequence of human inhabitance from the Neolithic period to the Islamic era. The region's strategic location along ancient trade routes, combined with its fertile soils and access to fresh water, made it an important area of human development throughout history.

### Prehistoric periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prehistoric periods")]

#### Neolithic and Chalcolithic periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Neolithic and Chalcolithic periods")]

The earliest findings of human settlement in the Sharon Plain dates to the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period. The most significant prehistoric site is ['En Esur](/wiki/%27En_Esur "'En Esur"), in the northern Sharon Plain at the entrance of the [Wadi Ara](/wiki/Wadi_Ara "Wadi Ara") pass.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This large, multi-level proto-historic site contains a sequence of settlements from the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") through [Early Chalcolithic](/wiki/Early_Chalcolithic "Early Chalcolithic"), [Late Chalcolithic](/wiki/Late_Chalcolithic "Late Chalcolithic") and Early Bronze Age periods. The site is in an area of surrounded by many springs and fertile ground, demonstrating early human adaptation to the region's environmental advantages.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Large scale [archaeological excavations](/wiki/Archaeological_excavation "Archaeological excavation") at ['En Esur](/wiki/%27En_Esur "'En Esur") and 'En Zippori in [Lower Galilee](/wiki/Lower_Galilee "Lower Galilee") have uncovered hundreds of sling stones dating to the Early Chalcolithic (5800–4500 BCE). The number of finds and standardisation in shape and size was interpreted by archaeologists as evidence of organised production, possibly centralised, and as a sign of warfare.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Bronze Age settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bronze Age settlements")]

#### Middle Bronze Age fortifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sharon_plain&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Middle Bronze Age fortifications")]

The Sharon Plain became heavily populated during the [Middle Bronze Age](/wiki/Middle_Bronze_Age "Middle Bronze Age"), with several major fortified cities established across the plain. Major cities included [Tel Afek](/wiki/Tel_Afek "Tel Afek"), [Tel Zeror](/wiki/Tel_Zeror "Tel Zeror"), [Tel Burga](/wiki/Tel_Burga "Tel Burga"), and Tel Poleg, which formed part a network of four fortified sites of the [Middle Bronze Age](/wiki/Middle_Bronze_Age "Middle Bronze Age") in the Sharon Plain.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Tel Zeror](/wiki/Tel_Zeror "Tel Zeror"), located approximately four kilometers east of modern [Hadera](/wiki/Hadera "Hadera"), was the first major tel in the Sharon Plain to undergo [archeological excavation](/wiki/Archaeological_excavation "Archaeological excavation"). Japanese expeditions in the 1960s uncovered a fortified, 50-dunam city with evidence of continuous human habitation throughout multiple ages.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
